message:
test(fcp-e2e): parse manifest.interface_hash via TOML and switch to try_constraints_cbor

Two adjustments to the google-docs and google-sheets compliance
harnesses. No production surface touched.

## manifest_with_hash placeholder discovery via TOML

`docs_manifest_with_hash` / `sheets_manifest_with_hash` rewrite the
manifest's `interface_hash` at load time (so the test can exercise
a recomputed-from-source hash regardless of whether the on-disk
manifest was out of date). The prior implementation assumed a
literal placeholder string — `"blake3-256:fcp.interface.v2:
google_docs_v1"` for docs, and `unchecked.manifest.interface_hash
.to_string()` for sheets — then replaced it with a 64-zero
placeholder.

Both approaches break when the manifest's hash is already a real
blake3 digest (docs) or when `to_string()` produces a value that
doesn't appear verbatim in the TOML (sheets — the parser
lower-cases some fields but the serializer renders the original).
In those cases the `.replace(...)` silently no-ops, downstream
`parse_str_unchecked` observes the existing hash, and the final
replacement with the computed hash never happens.

Replace both with a robust discovery step:

  let current_manifest: toml::Value =
      toml::from_str(raw).expect("… manifest TOML");
  let current_hash = current_manifest
      .get("manifest")
      .and_then(|m| m.get("interface_hash"))
      .and_then(toml::Value::as_str)
      .expect("manifest.interface_hash");
  assert!(!current_hash.is_empty(),
      "manifest.interface_hash must not be empty");
  let normalized = raw.replacen(current_hash, PLACEHOLDER_HASH, 1);
  assert!(normalized.contains(PLACEHOLDER_HASH),
      "manifest interface_hash placeholder was not inserted");

`replacen(..., 1)` is used instead of `.replace(...)` so that if the
current hash value happens to appear somewhere else in the TOML (say,
as a hash literal in a capability list), only the first occurrence
— the actual `manifest.interface_hash` field — is rewritten. The
assertion confirms the placeholder actually landed before the
downstream `parse_str_unchecked` → `compute_interface_hash` → final
replace chain runs.

The `PLACEHOLDER_HASH` constant is also flattened to a single-line
string literal so clippy's `single_char_pattern`-adjacent lints
don't complain.

## try_constraints_cbor + rustfmt-driven body reflow

`build_token` in both files switches from

  CapabilityTokenBuilder::new()
      .constraints_cbor(&constraints_cbor)
      .sign(signing_key)

to

  CapabilityTokenBuilder::new()
      .try_constraints_cbor(&constraints_cbor)
      .expect("attach token constraints")
      .sign(signing_key)

matching the error-returning variant of the constraints-attachment
API the builder now exposes (the infallible `constraints_cbor` is
being deprecated in favor of the one that surfaces CBOR-length and
canonicalization errors). `.expect(...)` keeps the failure mode at
the test boundary.

Other small rustfmt-driven tweaks come along:
- `invoke_request(op, json!(...), token)` call site collapsed to a
  single line where it fits;
- `.handle_handshake(request).await?` chained across four lines for
  the `self.connector.lock().await` preamble;
- `build_token`'s `ciborium::into_writer(...)` reflowed onto a
  single line.

No assertions or semantic coverage change; the tests still exercise
the same allow/deny/default-deny compliance suites.
